title: Can a brother act as wali for his sister's marriage if their father unjustly refuses?
stanceSummary: A previously unmarried woman requires her wali's (primary: father's) consent in the majority position (Hanbali, Shafi'i, Maliki). However, every rule has exceptions: if the wali is being **unjust or unreasonable** — e.g., refusing based purely on ethnicity when the suitor is otherwise a good Muslim — a **higher authority** (Islamic court, or in the West, a respected scholar) may transfer the *wilaya* to another male relative such as the brother. The process: work through and on the father first; involve trusted community elders; bring in a respected sheikh. Only as a last resort, when the father remains adamant in clear injustice, may a qualified scholar transfer the wilaya.
alternateQuestions: Can my brother do my nikah if my father refuses?
What can a woman do if her wali refuses her marriage unfairly?
Is it allowed to bypass the wali if he is being unjust?
What is the Islamic ruling on a father blocking his daughter's marriage based on ethnicity?
Can a sheikh transfer wilaya from an unjust wali?
transcript:
## Summary of Yasir Qadhi's Position

In a [2021 Ask Shaykh YQ video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=59LPrg4s69E) (starting at [0:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=59LPrg4s69E&t=0s)), Yasir Qadhi addresses what to do when a father unjustly refuses his daughter's marriage.

- Yasir Qadhi holds that a **previously unmarried woman requires her wali's approval** (majority position: Hanbali, Shafi'i, Maliki) — this is not overridden simply by cultural arguments or the woman's age
- A **divorced or widowed woman**: the wali's role becomes a formality; she effectively manages her own marriage
- **Every rule has exceptions**: if a wali is being demonstrably unjust (refusing purely on ethnicity, blocking a genuinely suitable Muslim match), a **qualified Islamic authority** may transfer the wilaya
- In the West, this means going to a **respected sheikh of the community** (ideally of the same ethnic background as the family, for maximum credibility with the father)
- **The proper process**: (1) exhaust all family channels — pressure from mother, brother, uncles, cousins; (2) bring in a community sheikh; (3) only as a last resort, have the sheikh transfer wilaya to the brother or another male relative
- The brother should **not** unilaterally declare himself wali without going through this process — it creates long-term family damage
- Context matters: spinsterhood is a genuine crisis in Western Muslim communities; refusing a good match for superficial reasons is itself a form of *zulm* (injustice) to one's daughter

---

## Full Lecture Transcript (Cleaned)

### The Question — [0:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=59LPrg4s69E&t=0s)

A brother from New Jersey writes that his elder sister has been trying to get married for many years and is now in her 30s. A good Muslim man has proposed — he is a suitable candidate — but their father refuses the marriage because the man is from a different ethnicity, and also because the sister met him at work, which the father finds Islamically improper. The brother asks: may he disobey his father and take over as wali for his sister?

Yasir Qadhi immediately notes he is speaking generically and is not pronouncing a specific verdict about this particular family, since he has only heard one side.

### Three Opinions on the Wali — [2:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=59LPrg4s69E&t=120s)

**Position 1 (Default of Hanbali, Shafi'i, Maliki)**: A wali is required for all women — previously unmarried or divorced — and the wali has an absolute right of veto.

**Position 2 (Found in Hanbali and Shafi'i)**: A previously unmarried woman requires her wali's full approval. A divorced or widowed woman's wali is a formality — she manages the marriage herself and the wali merely participates symbolically.

**Position 3 (Hanafi default)**: An adult woman does not need a wali's approval regardless of her marital history — she may conduct her own marriage like any other transaction.

Yasir Qadhi holds **Position 2** — previously unmarried women need their wali's approval, while divorced/widowed women effectively manage their own marriages.

### Why the Wali Rule Exists — [5:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=59LPrg4s69E&t=300s)

The purpose of the wali is not to impugn the woman's intelligence or judgment. It is to act as a **checks and balances** mechanism for the suitor. When a man knows a wali is involved, it immediately weeds out those who are not serious — Yasir Qadhi estimates the mere presence of a wali eliminates 90% of problematic suitors before any screening even begins. The wali understands men in a way that a woman who is in the early stages of emotional attachment may not.

### Every Rule Has Exceptions — [8:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=59LPrg4s69E&t=480s)

The default rule remains: an unmarried woman needs her wali's approval. But the wali himself has a higher authority above him: the Islamic court (or in the West, the respected community scholar). Just as the wali acts as checks and balances for the suitor, the court acts as checks and balances for the wali.

If the wali is being unjust — refusing a genuinely suitable match for petty reasons like ethnicity — a qualified authority may **transfer the wilaya** to another male relative.

### The Practical Process in the West — [10:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=59LPrg4s69E&t=600s)

1. **Work with and on the father first.** Do not jump over him immediately. Use family pressure: the mother, uncles, cousins, respected family friends. Soften him with emotional and rational arguments. Even a grudging "yes" is a yes.

2. **Bring in a community sheikh.** This sheikh should ideally be from the same ethnic background as the family, so the father will listen. The sheikh speaks to all parties — the potential groom (to verify his character), the sister, the brother, the mother, and then the father.

3. **Only as a last resort**, if the father remains absolutely adamant in clear injustice, the sheikh may transfer the wilaya to the brother.

4. **Why the brother should not act alone**: if the brother simply declares himself wali, the father may never forgive him. If the sheikh makes the decision, the father's anger is directed at a non-family member — and may eventually subside. Long-term family relationships matter.

### The Crisis of Spinsterhood — [13:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=59LPrg4s69E&t=780s)

Yasir Qadhi speaks plainly about a real crisis: in Western Muslim communities, the pool of suitable, practicing, educated, employed Muslim men is far smaller than the pool of suitable Muslim women. This is an acknowledged crisis. A father who blocks a good match for trivial reasons is committing *zulm* (injustice) against his daughter — and when the objection is purely ethnic, it has no basis in the Sharia.

Meeting at work is not a sin. Having feelings and wanting to get married is natural and praiseworthy. Our mother Khadijah (may Allah be pleased with her) herself facilitated the proposal to the Prophet ﷺ — there is precedent in the Seerah for women being proactive in this regard, as long as everything remained within the bounds of propriety.

And Allah knows best.
